ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for vegetarian black olive fried rice; broader 'fried rice food product' (FOODON_00002694) used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FAN (Fried rice) used as the closest defensible category; no specific code exists for vegetarian black olive fried rice variant.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for this dish; not assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of vegetarian black olive fried rice weighs approximately 400 g, typical of a single-person restaurant portion in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for vegetarian black olive fried rice exists in the HPB database. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore fried rice reference entries (plain fried rice and vegetable fried rice) combined with USDA FoodData Central data for black olives (USDA SR Legacy #09193) to account for the olive component. The input record's per-100g values (energy 162.5 kcal, protein 3.0 g, fat 6.25 g, carbohydrate 23.75 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained. Sodium at 300 mg/100 g is plausible given soy sauce and olive content. All values treated as medium-confidence estimates.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for vegetarian black olive fried rice. GI of 72 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white jasmine rice fried rice (GI ~72, high category), which is the dominant carbohydrate component. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 95.0) / 100 = 68.4, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 23.75 × 4 = 95.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 300 mg/100 g (1200 mg per 400 g plate) with low potassium offset (~160 mg/100 g) and low dietary fibre (1.25 g/100 g); black olives and soy sauce are primary sodium contributors.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a plate, request reduced soy sauce, and ask for fewer black olives or rinsed olives to reduce sodium intake toward the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI jasmine rice (GI ~72) with 23.75 g carbohydrate per 100 g and only 1.25 g dietary fibre provides minimal glycaemic buffering, resulting in a high glycaemic load of 68.4 per standard 400 g plate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (150–200 g), request brown rice as a lower-GI substitute if available, and pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to slow glucose absorption and blunt post-meal blood glucose spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is 6.25 g/100 g with saturated fat at 1.0 g/100 g (low); monounsaturated fat from black olives (~3.5 g/100 g estimated) is cardioprotective, but sodium at 300 mg/100 g (1200 mg per plate) is a concern for blood pressure management in CVD patients.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The favourable fat profile from black olives (high monounsaturated fat, low saturated fat) is beneficial for cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content per plate warrants portion control and reduced-sodium preparation for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Black olives provide oleic acid and polyphenols with anti-inflammatory properties; saturated fat is low at 1.0 g/100 g; however, high sodium from olives and soy sauce may worsen RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory monounsaturated fats from black olives, but patients should request fewer olives and reduced soy sauce to limit sodium-driven fluid retention and inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Black Olive Fried Rice is a Thai-influenced stir-fried rice dish served in Singapore, featuring cooked jasmine rice wok-tossed with cured black olives, mixed vegetables, garlic, and soy sauce. The dish is entirely plant-based, making it suitable for vegetarian and vegan diners, and is typically halal-certified in Singapore hawker and restaurant contexts. The black olives impart a distinctive savoury, briny, and slightly bitter flavour that contrasts with the mild sweetness of the rice and vegetables, while garlic and soy sauce provide aromatic depth. The texture is characteristic of wok-fried rice: slightly chewy grains with caramelised edges from high-heat stir-frying. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides approximately 162.5 kcal, 3.0 g protein, 6.25 g fat (predominantly monounsaturated from olives), and 23.75 g carbohydrate with 1.25 g dietary fibre. Sodium is elevated at 300 mg/100 g due to soy sauce and olive brine, making the dish a moderate concern for hypertension. The high glycaemic index (~72) of jasmine rice and the high glycaemic load per plate (GL ~68) make this dish a high concern for Type 2 Diabetes management. The monounsaturated fat content from black olives is cardioprotective, though the sodium level warrants caution for cardiovascular disease patients. This dish is commonly ordered as a single-plate meal in Thai restaurants and food courts across Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for vegetarian black olive fried rice exists in the HPB Singapore or USDA FoodData Central databases. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore vegetable fried rice reference data combined with USDA SR Legacy black olive data. The input record's values are consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained with medium confidence. GI is estimated from the analogous jasmine rice fried rice entry in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from HPB Singapore fried rice reference entries and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy black olive data (USDA #09193); no exact match exists in any single approved source for this specific dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (3.5) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.2) are estimated from the olive and vegetable oil component proportions using USDA SR Legacy data; these are not directly reported in HPB for this dish. 3. potassium_mg (160.0) is estimated from component analysis; not directly reported in HPB for this dish. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific Thai-style vegetarian black olive fried rice preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry as of 2024. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; dietary cholesterol is naturally zero. 6. The dish is sold under menu code 613D at a Thai restaurant on GrabFood Singapore. 7. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on jasmine rice fried rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 8. Ethnic tags 'Curry' and 'Pad Thai' from the input record have been removed as they are not applicable to this dish.
